elements.sent-2411-001: The typical selective state-space model (SSM) of Mamba addresses several limitations of Transformers, such as quadratic computational complexity with sequence length and significant inference-time memory requirements due to the key-value cache.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the growing size of Mamba models continues to pose training and deployment challenges and raises environmental concerns due to considerable energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we introduce \bimamba, a scalable and powerful 1-bit Mamba architecture designed for more efficient large language models with multiple sizes across 780M, 1.3B, and 2.7B.
elements.sent-2411-004: Extensive experimental results on language modeling demonstrate that \bimamba achieves performance comparable to its full-precision counterparts (e.g., FP16 or BF16) and much better accuracy than post-training-binarization (PTB) Mamba baselines, while significantly reducing memory footprint and energy consumption compared to the original Mamba model.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our study pioneers a new linear computational complexity LLM framework under low-bit representation and facilitates the future design of specialized hardware tailored for efficient 1-bit Mamba-based LLMs.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/Trend.pdf} \caption{Perplexity comparison of \bimamba, GPTQ and Bi-LLM on Wiki2 dataset. GPTQ and Bi-LLM show significant performance degradation when the bit is low. \bimamba demonstrates low perplexity in 1 bit and shows similar performance as GPTQ-8bit.} \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-006: The Selective State-Space Model (SSM) \citep{DBLP:conf/nips/GuJGSDRR21, DBLP:conf/iclr/GuGR22 has recently emerged as a powerful alternative to Transformers \cite{transformers in language modeling, demonstrating comparable or superior performance at small to moderate scales.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: SSMs are characterized by linear scaling in sequence length during training and a constant state size during generation, which significantly reduces computational and memory overhead, making them more efficient in terms of speed and memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-008: The representative SSM model of Mamba \citep{mamba, mamba-2 has a significant advantage when handling long context sequences due to its linear complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-009: In contrast, conventional transformers suffer from quadratic complexity as the sequence length increases.
elements.sent-2411-010: This means that for tasks involving large input sequences or extended contexts, Mamba is much more efficient, as its memory and computational requirements scale linearly with the sequence length.
elements.sent-2411-011: In practice, this allows Mamba to process long sequences faster and with lower resource consumption, making it ideal for applications such as long document processing, conversational agents, and any scenario where managing large amounts of contextual information is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-012: On the other hand, transformers require exponentially more resources as the context length increases, which can quickly become a bottleneck in long-context tasks.
elements.sent-2411-013: Prior works on Transformers have been extensively studied for several years \cite{BERT, radford2019language, touvron2023llamaopenefficientfoundation, achiam2023gpt , with numerous methods proposed to alleviate their high computational and storage costs, such as pruning \citep{ma2023llmprunerstructuralpruninglarge , model quantization \cite{, gptq , and KV Cache \citep{DBLP:conf/mlsys/PopeDCDBHXAD23, DBLP:conf/sosp/KwonLZ0ZY0ZS23 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Among these methods, quantization has proven to be highly effective \cite{dettmers2022llmint88bitmatrixmultiplication .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Researchers have successfully quantized transformer models from 16-bit to 8-bit, 4-bit, and even 1-bit representations, often with minimal performance degradation \cite{gptq, ma2024era1bitllmslarge, ma2024fbillm .}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, there has been little investigation into how SSM models or Mamba behave after low-bit quantization or even binarization.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this paper, we introduce \bimamba, a novel approach that applies extreme quantization to SSM models by reducing weights to a binary setting.
elements.sent-2411-018: Through this extreme quantization, we demonstrate that SSM models can be effectively binarized for both training and inference, maintaining high performance while significantly reducing memory footprint and energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our work pioneers a new framework for 1-bit representation in linear computational complexity models, potentially facilitating the development of specialized hardware tailored for fully low-bit SSM-based language models.
elements.sent-2411-020: We provide comprehensive experimental results showing that \bimamba achieves competitive performance comparing to full-precision counterpart LLMs, thereby establishing the feasibility and benefits of binarizing SSM models.
elements.sent-2411-021: To further understand the behaviors of binarization on Mambas, we conducted extensive empirical studies to analyze the distribution of pre-trained and binarized weights in Mambas.
elements.sent-2411-022: The results of this study are detailed in the experiments and Appendix sections, leading to two key observations:.
elements.sent-2411-023: As shown in \figref{fig:bi-mamba , post-training-binarization methods like BiLLM~\cite{wei2024billm and PB-LLM~\cite{shang2023pb typically tend to shift the distribution of weights on Mamba after binarization, resulting in a misaligned distribution to the optimal binary weights.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Our binarization-aware training, however, ensures that the binarized weights remain close to the original weight distribution, preserving the largest capability in weight representation binarization.
elements.sent-2411-025: Based on our empirical experiments, applying existing LLM post-binarization methods~\cite{gptq,wei2024billm , even when retaining salient weights, often severely degrades the performance of the Mamba model.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Without accounting for salient weights, binarization-aware training appears to be the only feasible solution for effectively binarizing models like Mamba while preserving competitive performance.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our contributions in this paper are as follows: (1) This is the first work to successfully binarize the Mamba architecture to 1-bit while maintaining strong performance.

Compared with the post-binarization method (Bi-LLM), our binarization-aware training method (\bimamba) generates a more similar weight distribution (after scaling) on each part. } \label{fig:bi-mamba}
elements.sent-2411-028: Due to their large parameter count, large language models (LLMs)~\citep{brown2020gpt3,openai2023gpt4,touvron2023llamaopenefficientfoundation are resource intensive to run.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Therefore they are often quantized to low bits representations during inference.
elements.sent-2411-030: This type of quantization is typically called post-training quantization (PTQ)~\citep{dettmers2022llmint8,xiao2023smoothquant,wei2022outlier,gptq,yao2022zeroquant,xiao2023smoothquant,lin2024awq , where the quantization operation is applied to the pretrained models.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Post-training quantization methods of LLMs are typically based on the empirical observations that a small set of salient features in pretrained Transformers have significantly large magnitudes, and quantization of these features needs to be extra careful~\citep{xiao2023smoothquant,lin2023awq .}
elements.sent-2411-032: PTQ methods have led to near lossless accuracy drop for INT8 weights and activations quantization, and INT4 weight-only quantization.
elements.sent-2411-033: While many works~\citep{wei2024billm,egiazarian2024extreme,shao2023omniquant,chee2023quip,tseng2024quip have been focused on pushing post-training weight quantization below 4 bits, they often leads to drastic drop of model performance.. Quantization-Aware Training.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Different from post-training quantization, quantization-aware training (QAT) aims to learn the quantized models during training.
elements.sent-2411-035: In the LLM era, QAT is less investigated as compared to PTQ because LLMs typically require huge amounts of compute resources to train.
elements.sent-2411-036: However, these methods still require the pretrained weights to initialize the student LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-037: Most recently, \citet{ma2024era1bitllmslarge trained LLMs with tenary weights from random initializations, achieving non-trivial performance.}
elements.sent-2411-038: While LLMs are often built with Transformers~\citep{transformers , their self-attention operations suffers from quadratic time complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-039: This makes Transformers inefficient to run on long sequences.
elements.sent-2411-040: Therefore there have been huge research efforts aiming at addressing this issue~\citep{peng2023rwkv,beck2024xlstm,katharopoulos2020linearrnn,de2024griffin .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Among these efforts, State-space models (SSMs)~\cite{mamba,mamba-2,gu2023s4 are a type of recurrent neural networks.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The latest advanced SSMs, e.g., Mamba~\cite{mamba and Mamba-2~\cite{mamba-2 have demonstrated superior performance as compared to Transformers, while having linear complexity with respect to the sequence length.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Despite their promising capabilities, how to effectively quantize this architecture has been rarely investigated.

elements.sent-2411-045: Mamba belongs to a class of models known as State Space Models (SSMs), which is able to offer performance and scaling laws comparable to the Transformer while remaining practical at extremely long sequence lengths (e.g., one million tokens).
elements.sent-2411-046: It achieves this extended context by eliminating the ``quadratic bottleneck'' present in the attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-047: The vanilla structured state space sequence models (S4) transform a 1-dimensional sequence $x\in R^T$ into another sequence $y\in R^T$ by utilizing an latent state $h \in R^{T, N}$ as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:s4} \begin{aligned} h_t=Ah_{t-1}+Bx_t y_t=Ch_t+Dx_t \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $A\in R^{N, N}$, $B\in R^{N, 1}$, $C\in R^{1, N}$, $D\in R$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Since the parameters in Equation \ref{eq:s4 are constant through time, this model is linear time-invariant (LTI).}
elements.sent-2411-049: The LTI model gives equal attention to all elements when processing sequences, which prevents the model from effectively understanding natural language.
elements.sent-2411-050: To address this, Mamba improved it to the Selective State Space Model, where $B$ and $C$ are obtained through a linear projection of $x_t$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Meanwhile, the above equation applies to dynamic systems with continuous input and output signals.
elements.sent-2411-052: So, it needs to do discretization when dealing with text sequences: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \overline{A}=exp(\Delta A) \overline{B}=exp(\Delta A)^{-1}(exp(\Delta A) - I) \cdot \Delta B \end{aligned}\end{equation} where, $\Delta$ is related to $x_t$ and a learnable parameter $\Delta_{bias}$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Then, the calculating process of selective SSM is as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} h_t=\overline{A}h_{t-1}+\overline{B}x_t y_t=Ch_t+Dx_t \end{aligned}\end{equation} Centered around selective SSM, combined with linear projection for input and output along with layer normalization, this forms the basic block for Mamba.
elements.sent-2411-054: Compared to Mamba, Mamba-2 further replaces selective SSM with the state space duality (SSD).
elements.sent-2411-055: In SSD, $A$ is simplified as scalar-times-identity structure, and SSD uses a larger head dimension which is equal to 1 in Mamba.
elements.sent-2411-056: Meanwhile, Mamba-2 block also introduces simplifications, such as removing sequential linear projections in Mamba block, to facilitate parallel training.
elements.sent-2411-057: The basic structure of Mamba-2 is shown in \figref{fig:bi-mamba .}
elements.sent-2411-058: To begin with, we need to identify what weight matrices can be binarized in Mamba architecture.
elements.sent-2411-059: We use the latest Mamba-2~\cite{mamba-2 as our base architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-060: According to the description above, we can interpret the SSD matrices of $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$, and $\Delta_{bias}$ more intuitively, as well as other layers including {\em embedding, layer normalization (LN), Conv-1d, inner linear projection, out linear projection in Mamba-2 to better understand the effect after binarization, so that to determine the binarization space.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Briefly, $A$ is the transition state matrix, representing how the current state transitions to the next state.
elements.sent-2411-062: It answers the question of {\em how should the model gradually forget the less relevant parts of the state over time? $B$ maps the new input to the state, addressing the point of {\em which parts of the new input should the model retain? $C$ maps the state to the output of the SSM, asking that {\em how can the model utilize the current state to make an accurate next prediction? $D$ shows how the new input directly influences the output, acting as a modified skip connection and asking {\em how can the model incorporate the new input into the prediction? Considering our base architecture Mamba-2, we present the specific parameters proportion for different layers across different sizes as shown in \figref{fig:bi-mamba and \tabref{tab:proportion .\footnote{Since $B$ and $C$ are derived from the linear projection of each layer’s inputs, and the language model head is tied to the embedding layer, these modules are ignored in our design.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: It is observed that, in Mamba-2, the vast majority of the model's parameters are in the linear modules, excluding the causal head.
elements.sent-2411-064: For instance, in Mamba-2-2.7B, these parameters account for 95.2\% of the entire model.
elements.sent-2411-065: Additionally, the embedding module shares parameters with the causal head.
elements.sent-2411-066: Binarizing the embedding significantly diminishes its capability to represent token semantics, thereby reducing model performance.
elements.sent-2411-067: Therefore, in our \bimamba, we only binarize the parameters in the linear modules (excluding the causal head).
elements.sent-2411-068: This strategy aims to maintain a high compression ratio of 90\% while still allowing the binarized model to perform effectively.
elements.sent-2411-069: To binarize Mamba, we replace the original linear modules with the FBI-Linear module introduced by FBI-LLM~\cite{ma2024fbillm .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Specifically, the FBI-Linear module primarily consists of two parts: a matrix $\boldsymbol{W ^b \in\mathbb{R ^{m\times n}$ made up solely of $\{1, -1\}$ and high-precision scale factors $\boldsymbol{\alpha \in \mathbb{R ^n$ and $\boldsymbol{\beta \in \mathbb{R ^n$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The inference process of FBI-Linear is as follows: \begin{equation}\label{fbi-linear} \boldsymbol{y} = \widetilde{\boldsymbol{W}}^b\boldsymbol{x}\end{equation} where $\widetilde{\boldsymbol{W ^b$ is derived by performing column-wise multiplication with $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ and addition with $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ respectively: \begin{equation}\widetilde{\boldsymbol{W}}^b_{\cdot,i} = \alpha_i\boldsymbol{W}^b_{\cdot,i}+\beta_i\end{equation} where $\alpha_i$ and $\beta_i$ are the learnable scaling and shifting factors at $i$-th layer.}}

elements.sent-2411-100: For the 780M Mamba-2 model, \bimamba demonstrates an average downstream performance of 44.5, outperforming GPTQ-3bit and Bi-LLM, which achieve 42.6 and 38.1, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-101: In perplexity assessments, \bimamba reports scores of 14.2, 34.4, and 15.0 on Wiki2, PTB, and C4, respectively, with the baseline models exhibiting up to 10$\times$ higher perplexity.
elements.sent-2411-102: For the 1.3B model, \bimamba achieves a notable downstream accuracy of 45.8 on average, surpassing GPTQ-2bit’s 35.3 and Bi-LLM’s 36.2.
elements.sent-2411-103: This performance indicates Bi-Mamba's enhanced generalization across a wider range of tasks at this model size.
elements.sent-2411-104: Additionally, \bimamba demonstrates substantially improved perplexity, registering scores of 13.2, 30.8, and 14.0 on Wiki2, PTB, and C4 datasets, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-105: In comparison, GPTQ-2bit and Bi-LLM present considerably higher perplexity values, underscoring the efficiency of Bi-Mamba's binarization in maintaining linguistic coherence.
elements.sent-2411-106: For the 2.7B model, \bimamba further extends its lead, achieving an average downstream accuracy of 49.3, compared to 35.8 for GPTQ-2bit and 38.5 for Bi-LLM.
elements.sent-2411-107: Notably, \bimamba maintains low perplexity across all datasets, with scores of 10.7, 24.2, and 11.9 on Wiki2, PTB, and C4, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-108: These results highlight Bi-Mamba's ability to retain high-level performance in both task accuracy and linguistic fluency as model complexity scales up.
elements.sent-2411-109: In summary, \bimamba consistently demonstrates superior zero-shot performance and perplexity reduction across model sizes, substantiating its robustness and versatility compared to GPTQ and Bi-LLM, particularly in larger, more complex models.

elements.sent-2411-119: Moving to the downstream task evaluation, we first observe the catastrophic performance degradation of the binarized models, whose performance is even lower than the random results on many benchmarks such as the results on ARC-E and ARC-C.

elements.sent-2411-121: However, after binarization-aware training, the model recovers the performance on all benchmarks and finally outperforms all baselines including GPTQ-3bit, GPT-2bit and Bi-LLM.
elements.sent-2411-122: This underscores the importance of binarization-aware training in achieving competitive results.
elements.sent-2411-123: In this section, we explore the binarization space of Mamba models and discuss the effect of binarizing each part.
elements.sent-2411-124: We conduct experiments that binarize the In\_Proj and Out\_Proj separately and binarize both.
elements.sent-2411-125: The binarized models are trained with the same data.
elements.sent-2411-127: Partial and full binarization are compared with the full-precision Mamba model on perplexity and downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-128: First, the results in the table show that partial binarization generally retains higher zero-shot accuracy compared to full binarization, though it still shows some performance degradation compared to the original Mamba-2 models.
elements.sent-2411-129: However, the performance gap between the partial and full binarized models is not significant.
elements.sent-2411-130: For instance, in the Mamba-2-2.7B model, partial binarization achieves an average accuracy of 53.1, while full binarization reduces this to 49.3.
elements.sent-2411-131: Across all model sizes, partial-binarized \bimamba consistently outperforms full-binarized \bimamba on most benchmarks, reflecting that partial binarization can mitigate some of the accuracy loss associated with full binarization.
elements.sent-2411-132: It also suggests that while partial binarization preserves a slight edge, full binarization remains highly competitive and does not substantially lag.
elements.sent-2411-133: In terms of perplexity, the fully binarized model also performs comparably to the partial model.
elements.sent-2411-134: For example, in the Mamba-2-780M model, the C4 dataset perplexity for full-binarized \bimamba (Fully) is 15.0, compared to 14.4 for partial-binarized \bimamba, demonstrating that full binarization does not impose a significant perplexity increase.
elements.sent-2411-135: These findings highlight that the fully binarized model can maintain competitive performance with the partial binarization model, particularly in terms of perplexity, while still benefiting from greater storage and computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-136: The results suggest that full binarization, contrary to initial expectations, does not substantially compromise model effectiveness, offering a viable, efficient alternative.

elements.sent-2411-143: We introduced \bimamba, a scalable and efficient 1-bit Mamba architecture designed for large language models in multiple sizes: 780M, 1.3B, and 2.7B parameters.
elements.sent-2411-144: We begin by identifying the binarization space within the Mamba architecture.
elements.sent-2411-145: Then, \bimamba models are trained from scratch on large datasets, similar to standard LLM pretraining, using an autoregressive distillation loss.
elements.sent-2411-146: Extensive language modeling experiments show that \bimamba achieves competitive performance that is slightly lower than its full-precision counterparts (e.g., FP16 or BF16), while substantially reduces memory usage and computational cost compared to the original precision Mamba.
elements.sent-2411-147: This study provided a novel, first accessible and low-bit framework with linear computational complexity, laying the foundation for developing specialized hardware optimized for efficient 1-bit Mamba-based LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-148: While \bimamba achieves competitive performance to full-precision models, there may still be trade-offs in accuracy, particularly in complex tasks that rely heavily on nuanced language understanding.
elements.sent-2411-149: Also, full deployment may require specialized hardware to maximize efficiency gains, limiting accessibility on standard hardware setups.
elements.sent-2411-150: On ethical part, reducing model precision could risk oversimplifying nuanced patterns in data, potentially amplifying biases present in the training data.
elements.sent-2411-151: Moreover, while \bimamba reduces energy consumption during inference, training binary models from scratch can still be computationally intensive.. \onecolumn .

elements.sent-2411-171: We visualized the weight distributions of different modules in Mamba-2 (Orange histograms) and \bimamba (Blue histograms), as shown in \figref{fig:1stdistribution , \ref{fig:distribution_24layer and \ref{fig:distribution_last .}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: We visualize the weight parameter distributions of different modules in the first (1st), mid (24th) and final (48th) layers of the corresponding 780M models.
elements.sent-2411-173: The input and output projection matrices are the values after re-scaling.
elements.sent-2411-174: Each pair of histograms compares how \bimamba modifies the distribution of weights in different modules, no matter whether the module is binarized or not, illustrating the impact of \bimamba on each module.
elements.sent-2411-175: Specifically, in the first layer, the weight distribution of the original Mamba-2 such as Conv1d.weight, Conv1d.bias and D are tightly concentrated, indicating the strong focus on specific values.
elements.sent-2411-176: In contrast, the weight distribution in \bimamba in the first layer is much divergent with additional peaks in the histograms such as in A-log, Conv1d.bias and D.
elements.sent-2411-177: The divergent weight distributions in \bimamba suggest that \bimamba intentionally captures broader values in the initial layers to retain sufficient information for binarized modules.
elements.sent-2411-178: With more variability at the initial layers, \bimamba can process the diverse initial features even in low-bit precision.
elements.sent-2411-179: In the mid-depth layers such as the 24th layer, the weight distribution of both the original Mamba and \bimamba show similar patterns as in the first layer.
elements.sent-2411-180: However, the divergence is more moderate compared with the divergence in the first layer.
elements.sent-2411-181: This suggests that in the intermediate layers, \bimamba can refine the intermediate representation with generalization with binarized weights.
elements.sent-2411-182: Finally, in the last layer, the divergence patterns also remain while the distribution is much narrower compared with previous layers, reflecting a more concentrated range of values.
elements.sent-2411-183: The focused distribution helps to model to generate a stable and reliable final representation.
elements.sent-2411-184: In all, our \bimamba includes a much wider distribution to capture more information at the beginning stages while the distribution tends to be more centralized progressively to output stable final results.

elements.sent-2411-185: We provide generation cases from our models in different scales including 2.7B, 1.3B and 780M, and other baseline models including GPTQ-3bit, GPTQ-2bit and Bi-LLM as shown in \figref{fig:case1 , \ref{fig:case2 and \ref{fig:case3 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: It is observed that Mamba-2 consistently produces coherent answers with meaningful semantic information but often repeats the content excessively.
elements.sent-2411-187: Nevertheless, \bimamba is more robust than other baseline methods in preserving relevant information.
elements.sent-2411-188: Post-training quantization methods, particularly at lower bit levels (e.g., GPTQ-2bit and 1bit BiLLM), tend to produce meaningless or garbled content.
elements.sent-2411-189: Specifically, GPTQ-3bit occasionally provides coherent starts but quickly devolves into repetitive or nonsensical text, indicating limited content understanding and generation ability after quantization.
elements.sent-2411-190: Other low-bit settings such as GPTQ-2bit and Bi-LLM generally fail to maintain coherence for generation, resulting in meaningless symbols generation, especially in smaller models.
elements.sent-2411-191: The typical Selective State-Space Model (SSM) used in Mamba addresses several limitations of Transformers, such as the quadratic computational complexity with respect to sequence length and the significant memory requirements during inference due to the key-value (KV) cache.
elements.sent-2411-192: However, the increasing size of Mamba models continues to pose challenges for training and deployment, particularly due to their substantial computational demands during both training and inference.
elements.sent-2411-193: In this work, we introduce \bimamba, a scalable and powerful 1-bit Mamba architecture designed to enable more efficient large language models (LLMs), with model sizes of 780M, 1.3B, and 2.7B parameters.
elements.sent-2411-194: Extensive experiments on language modeling benchmarks demonstrate that \bimamba achieves performance comparable to its full-precision (FP16 or BF16) counterparts, while outperforming post-training binarization (PTB) Mamba and binarization-aware training (BAT) Transformer baselines.
elements.sent-2411-195: Moreover, \bimamba drastically reduces memory usage and computational cost compared to the original Mamba.
elements.sent-2411-196: Our work pioneers a new line of linear-complexity LLMs under low-bit representation and paves the way for the design of specialized hardware optimized for efficient 1-bit Mamba-based models.
elements.sent-2411-197: Code and the pre-trained weights are available at \href{https://github.com/Tangshengku/Bi-Mamba {https://github.com/Tangshengku/Bi-Mamba .}}
elements.sent-2411-198: The Selective State-Space Model (SSM) \citep{DBLP:conf/nips/GuJGSDRR21, DBLP:conf/iclr/GuGR22 has recently emerged as a powerful alternative to Transformers \citep{transformers in language modeling, achieving comparable or superior performance at small to moderate scales.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: SSMs scale linearly with sequence length during training and maintain a constant state size during generation, which significantly reduces computational and memory overhead, resulting in greater speed and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-200: The representative SSM model, Mamba \citep{mamba, mamba-2 , has a clear advantage in handling long-context sequences due to its linear complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-201: In contrast, conventional Transformers exhibit quadratic complexity as sequence length increases.
elements.sent-2411-202: This makes Mamba substantially more efficient for tasks involving large inputs or extended contexts, with memory and compute requirements that grow only linearly.
elements.sent-2411-203: In practice, this enables faster processing of long sequences with lower resource consumption, making Mamba well-suited for applications such as long-document processing, conversational agents, and other scenarios requiring large-context management.
elements.sent-2411-204: On the other hand, Transformers require polynomially more resources as context length grows, which quickly becomes a bottleneck in long-context tasks.
elements.sent-2411-205: Prior work \citep{BERT, radford2019language, touvron2023llamaopenefficientfoundation, achiam2023gpt on Transformers has been extensive, with numerous methods proposed to reduce their high computational and storage costs, such as pruning \citep{ma2023llmprunerstructuralpruninglarge , model quantization \citep{gptq , and KV cache optimizations \citep{DBLP:conf/mlsys/PopeDCDBHXAD23, DBLP:conf/sosp/KwonLZ0ZY0ZS23 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Among these, quantization has proven highly effective \citep{dettmers2022llmint88bitmatrixmultiplication , enabling reductions from 16-bit to 8-bit, 4-bit, and even 1-bit representations with minimal performance loss \citep{gptq, ma2024era, ma2024fbillm .}}
elements.sent-2411-207: However, little is known about how SSM models (Mamba in particular) perform under low-bit quantization or binarization.
elements.sent-2411-208: In this paper, we present \bimamba, a novel approach that applies extreme quantization to SSM models by reducing weights to a binary setting.

elements.sent-2411-261: HS, WG, and OBQA are abbreviations for HellaSwag, WinoGrande, and OpenbookQA, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-262: The visualization of performance comparison is shown in \Figref{fig:radar .}
elements.sent-2411-263: For the 780M Mamba-2 model, \bimamba demonstrates an average downstream performance of 45.3, outperforming GPTQ-3bit and Bi-LLM, which achieve 42.6 and 38.1, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-264: In perplexity assessments, \bimamba reports scores of 13.4, 32.4, and 14.5 on Wiki2, PTB, and C4, respectively, with the baseline models exhibiting up to 10$\times$ higher perplexity.
elements.sent-2411-265: SqueezeLLM and AQLM achieve better performance than GPTQ on both downstream tasks and perplexity, yet they are still outperformed by the \bimamba 780M model.
elements.sent-2411-266: Moreover, \bimamba 780M surpasses the binarization-aware training method, BitNet in the zero-shot performance on downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-267: BitNet obtains 44.3 scores on average while \bimamba 780M achieves 45.3 scores.
elements.sent-2411-268: Additionally, \bimamba demonstrates substantially improved perplexity, registering scores of 11.7, 29.9, and 12.9 on Wiki2, PTB, and C4 datasets, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-269: Notably, \bimamba maintains low perplexity across all datasets, with scores of 10.0, 21.9, and 11.3 on Wiki2, PTB, and C4, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-270: While BitNet-1.58bit does not provide each perplexity respectively, the average perplexity of the 3B model is 9.91 on C4 and Wikitext2, which is reported in their original paper with 8-bit activation and 1.58-bit weights.
elements.sent-2411-271: In contrast, \bimamba keeps 16-bit activation and binarizes weights, obtaining 10.65 on average.
elements.sent-2411-272: BitNet-1.58b shows slightly better perplexity, which we attribute to its larger size—3B parameters compared to Bi-Mamba's 2.7B—and its higher precision at 1.58 bits.
elements.sent-2411-273: Moreover, OneBit on the transformer model only obtains 49.5 scores on average with 6.7B parameters, and BitNet gains 50.2 scores with 3.0B parameters.
elements.sent-2411-274: On the Mamba 780M with the 1.3B model, Bi-Mamba achieves better performance in both perplexity and downstream tasks; however, on the 2.7B model, Bi-Mamba shows improved perplexity but obtains comparable downstream task performance to Onebit.
elements.sent-2411-275: Bi-Mamba’s lower perplexity shows stronger language modeling, but this does not always yield higher task accuracy—especially when datasets’ knowledge coverage limits model capability.
elements.sent-2411-276: For results on HumanEval and GSM8K, we notice that the full-precision models across all sizes perform poorly, producing results close to random guessing.
elements.sent-2411-277: Other quantization methods further degrade performance compared to their full-precision counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-278: In contrast, our proposed Bi-Mamba consistently achieves superior performance, outperforming all other methods, including the full-precision model.
elements.sent-2411-279: We believe the reason is the higher quality of pretraining data.
elements.sent-2411-280: Bi-Mamba applied Amber dataset, which is a high-quality dataset compared with Pile dataset used in the original Mamba model.
elements.sent-2411-281: We further conduct the instruction tuning on our base Bi-Mamba model with OpenOrca \citep{OpenOrca dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-283: After instruction-tuning, the performance is further improved, demonstrating that Bi-Mamba can serve as a strong base model.
elements.sent-2411-284: Overall, the results of the Mamba models and their quantized versions are not highly competitive.
elements.sent-2411-285: The primary reasons for this are: 1) Limited Training Data.
elements.sent-2411-286: The Mamba-2-2.7B pre-trained model is trained with only 300B tokens on the Pile dataset.
elements.sent-2411-287: This amount of training data is relatively small compared to state-of-the-art models, which are often trained on significantly larger corpora (more than 10T tokens).
elements.sent-2411-288: As a result, the pre-trained model may not have fully developed strong reasoning and problem-solving capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-289: After the quantization or binarization, the reasoning ability collapses completely.
elements.sent-2411-290: The models in this evaluation have not been instruction-tuned, which is critical for performance on complex reasoning and coding benchmarks such as GSM8K and HumanEval.
elements.sent-2411-291: Instruction tuning enhances a model’s ability to follow prompts effectively and generalize across tasks.
elements.sent-2411-292: Quantizing an instructed model could yield better performance on complex reasoning tasks.

elements.sent-2411-304: Instead of binarization, one can train small models with full precision from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-305: We add the performance comparison of \bimamba and small models pretrained with full precision, as shown in Table \ref{tab:full_small_model .}
elements.sent-2411-306: We utilize the official pretrained weight from Mamba2, including models with 130M and 370M parameters pretrained with 300B tokens.
elements.sent-2411-307: From the table, all \bimamba models—including the 780M, 1.3B, and 2.7B variants trained on only 105B tokens—outperform the 130M full-precision models in average performance.
elements.sent-2411-308: Specifically, Mamba-2 130M obtains 44.2 accuracy on downstream tasks, with perplexities of 20.0, 35.1, and 25.2 on the Wiki, PTB, and C4 datasets, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-309: In comparison, the smallest \bimamba model, with 780M parameters, achieves 45.3 accuracy on downstream tasks and perplexities of 13.4, 32.4, and 14.5 on Wiki, PTB, and C4, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-310: Moreover, \bimamba 1.3B and 2.7B models achieve higher average performance than the full-precision 370M Mamba-2 model.
elements.sent-2411-311: Full-precision Mamba-2 370M gains 48.4 on average on downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-312: The \bimamba 1.3B model outperforms the 370M Mamba-2 model, achieving 48.4 accuracy on downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-313: The results indicate that binarization with post-training is better than training a full-precision small model from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-314: Although \bimamba includes extra training efforts due to the distillation compared with the small model, the overall GPU hours are comparable with the model with similar parameter size trained from scratch because of the lower training token requirement.
elements.sent-2411-315: Furthermore, our work is centered on the deployment phase, where binarized models can yield substantial benefits in terms of memory footprint, storage size, and inference efficiency—especially in CPU- or edge-constrained environments.
elements.sent-2411-316: As shown in Table \ref{tab:resource , the fully binarized Bi-Mamba-2.7B model achieves 5$\times$ memory reduction, 3$\times$ faster throughput, and 3$\times$ lower energy consumption.}
elements.sent-2411-317: Training-time cost is higher, but this is a one-time investment, amortized over repeated inference runs—particularly in latency-sensitive or resource-constrained deployment scenarios.

elements.sent-2411-319: Model binarization can significantly reduce the storage requirement on disk.
elements.sent-2411-320: Following Bi-LLM \citep{wei2024billm , we provide the theoretical storage requirement for our \bimamba in different model sizes compared with full-precision models, as shown in \tabref{tab:stroge .}}

elements.sent-2411-322: This analysis highlights the efficiency of full binarization in significantly reducing storage requirements while maintaining the model parameter count, making it a highly storage-efficient alternative for large models.

elements.sent-2411-323: We deploy our \bimamba on the CPU of Mac M4 Pro Chip by implementing \bimamba with llama.cpp framework\footnote{\url{https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-324: The throughput is measured on text generation with 128 tokens generated.
elements.sent-2411-325: The computation is repeated and calculated over 5 runs with 8 CPU threads.
elements.sent-2411-326: For the energy computation, we utilize the asitop\footnote{\url{https://github.com/tlkh/asitop .}}
elements.sent-2411-327: The final energy is computed as the product of the average power consumption and the program’s running time.
elements.sent-2411-328: The memory requirements for each model are evaluated with 2,048 tokens and a batch size of 128.
elements.sent-2411-329: Notably, \bimamba{ reduces memory usage by more than 5$\times$ and energy consumption by 3$\times$, while increasing inference throughput to 3.16$\times$—from 24.59 to 77.81.}
elements.sent-2411-330: Moreover, energy consumption is reduced by more than 3$\times$, from 148.4 J to 44.9 J for 128-token generation.
elements.sent-2411-331: These improvements in resource efficiency highlight \bimamba's potential to significantly reduce computational costs in practice.

elements.sent-2411-335: In this section, we provide the ablation study of \bimamba.
elements.sent-2411-336: As shown in Table \ref{tab:ablation_full , we conduct various ablation studies in the 780M model including the model without knowledge distillation (w/o KD in the table), the model utilizing the KL divergence loss as distillation loss (KL Div in the table) and the model using a different teacher model (Phi-3.5-instruct-mini \citep{abdin2024phi ).}}
elements.sent-2411-337: The results demonstrate the importance of each component, as removing knowledge distillation (KD) or using alternate loss functions (KL Div and Phi-3.5) significantly reduces performance compared to the full Bi-Mamba model, which achieves the best results across all metrics except PTB.
elements.sent-2411-338: Moreover, the stronger performance with Llama2-7B as a teacher compared with Phi-3.5 as a teacher indicates that the better the teacher is, the higher performance the student will achieve.
elements.sent-2411-339: Specifically, Bi-mamba trained with the original autoregressive loss obtains the lowest performance compared with other models trained with KD.
elements.sent-2411-340: The average accuracy on downstream tasks is 42.7, which is surpassed by the model trained with KL-Div loss, namely 43.3.
elements.sent-2411-341: With a different teacher, Phi-3.5, \bimamba achieves similar performance as the model trained with Llama-2-7B as the teacher, demonstrating the effectiveness of our proposed autoregressive knowledge distillation.
elements.sent-2411-342: We introduce \bimamba, a scalable and efficient 1-bit Mamba architecture designed for large language models in multiple sizes: 780M, 1.3B, and 2.7B parameters.
elements.sent-2411-343: Extensive language modeling experiments show that \bimamba{ achieves competitive performance, only slightly lower than its full-precision counterparts (e.g., FP16 or BF16), while substantially reducing memory usage and computational cost compared to the original-precision Mamba.}
elements.sent-2411-344: Moreover, while \bimamba reduces energy consumption during inference, training binary models from scratch can still be computationally intensive.. \bibliographystyle{tmlr .}
elements.sent-2411-345: We present the performance dynamics of the 2.7B and 1.3B \bimamba models during training in terms of perplexity and multiple downstream tasks, as shown in \Figref{fig:2.7btraining_cost and \ref{fig:1.3btraining_cost .}}
elements.sent-2411-346: We provide the full results of the ablation study on downstream tasks, as shown in Table \ref{tab:ablation_full .}
elements.sent-2411-347: Without knowledge distillation, the model obtains the lowest average zero-shot accuracy on downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-348: With knowledge distillation, \bimamba achieves the highest accuracy on downstream tasks, demonstrating the effectiveness of our \bimamba.
